body_txt: The reactions of each party will reveal the truth: Alice will be distraught (usually begging the judge to simply give the object to Bob), and Bob will be unmoved . This marks her out as the rightful owner, since only the real owner would concede something precious to her rival rather than see it destroyed. In straight examples, only one party will object, because there is no middle ground between the rightful mother and a sociopath, or psychopath, who would rather have half a dead baby than let someone else raise a live one ; alternatively, the failure to object may simply be a case of calling the judge's bluff, but the fact that one party was willing to even take this risk while the other was not is generally sufficient proof in itself. In a common subversion, both claimants object, leaving the would-be Solomon stumped (though an especially clever judge could double-subvert this back into An Aesop about reaching an amicable compromise before allowing things to escalate). A common variation in logic puzzles is to have both parties already agreeing to split something that is inherently difficult to split fairly, but not being able to agree on the exact method. The most common solution is to have one party decide how to divide the lot and the other party choose which half to take — this gives the splitter every incentive to divide it as fairly as they can manage. Often subverted nowadays. Compare Spot the Imposter . Super-Trope of Solomon Divorce , where divorcing parents split their children up so there's at least one for each. See also No MacGuffin, No Winner and Tear-Apart Tug-of-War .
